flashcards.back: FTP uses a **control channel** for commands and responses, and a **data channel** for file transfers.

flashcards.front: Does FTP encrypt data by default?
flashcards.back: No, standard FTP is **unencrypted** by default, making it vulnerable to interception.

flashcards.front: What are the secure versions of FTP?
flashcards.back: **FTPS** and **SFTP** are secure versions of FTP that address encryption.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between IP and TCP?
flashcards.back: IP is connectionless and focuses on addressing and routing, while TCP is connection-oriented and ensures reliable data transmission.

flashcards.front: What layer does IP operate at?
flashcards.back: IP operates at the **network layer**.

flashcards.front: What layer does TCP operate at?
flashcards.back: TCP operates at the **transport layer**.

flashcards.front: What layer does FTP operate at?
flashcards.back: FTP operates at the **application layer**.

title: C.1.8 Components of a Web Page Flashcards

flashcards.front: What is a **web page**?
flashcards.back: A **digital document** accessible through a **web browser**, typically written in **HTML** and enhanced with **CSS** and **JavaScript**.

flashcards.front: What does **HTML** provide for a web page?
flashcards.back: **HTML** provides the **basic structure** of a web page using **tags**.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the **head** section in HTML?
flashcards.back: The **head** section contains **metadata** and **resources** that are not displayed directly but are essential for functionality.

flashcards.front: What does the **title tag** do?
flashcards.back: The **title tag** sets the title of the web page displayed in the browser tab.

flashcards.front: What is the role of **meta tags**?
flashcards.back: **Meta tags** provide information about the page, such as character set, author, and description.

flashcards.front: What does the **body** section of an HTML document contain?
flashcards.back: The **body** section contains the **visible content** of the web page.

flashcards.front: How are **headings** defined in HTML?
flashcards.back: **Headings** are defined by <h1> to <h6> tags, structuring content hierarchically.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of **CSS**?
flashcards.back: **CSS** is used to **style** and **layout** web pages.

flashcards.front: What are the three ways to apply **CSS**?
flashcards.back: **Inline styles**, **internal styles**, and **external styles**.

flashcards.front: What does **JavaScript** add to web pages?
flashcards.back: **JavaScript** adds **interactivity** and **dynamic behavior** to web pages.

flashcards.front: What are **meta tags** used for?
flashcards.back: **Meta tags** provide **metadata** about the web page, used by browsers and search engines.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of **alt text**?
flashcards.back: **Alt text** describes images for screen readers, enhancing accessibility.

flashcards.front: What is **semantic HTML**?
flashcards.back: **Semantic HTML** uses tags like <nav>, <header>, and <footer> to define the structure.

flashcards.front: What is a key challenge in web page design?
flashcards.back: **Cross-browser compatibility**: Ensuring web pages look and function the same across different browsers.

flashcards.front: What is **responsive design**?
flashcards.back: **Responsive design** adapts layouts for various screen sizes.

flashcards.front: Why is **performance optimization** important?
flashcards.back: Balancing rich media and interactivity with fast load times is essential.

flashcards.front: What is a critical aspect of **security** in web design?
flashcards.back: Protecting user data, especially in forms, to prevent breaches.

flashcards.front: What are **forms** used for in web pages?
flashcards.back: **Forms** collect **user input** and send it to a server for processing.

flashcards.front: What are the components of a form?
flashcards.back: **Input fields**, **labels**, and **buttons**.

flashcards.front: What is the role of **links** in web pages?
flashcards.back: **Links** connect web pages and resources.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between **inline scripts** and **external scripts**?
flashcards.back: **Inline scripts** are written directly within HTML tags, while **external scripts** are linked through separate JavaScript files.

flashcards.front: What is the significance of **accessibility features** in web design?
flashcards.back: Ensuring web pages are accessible to all users, including those with disabilities.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between **SEO** and **meta tags**?
flashcards.back: **Meta tags** improve a page's search engine ranking, making it easier to find.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of **link tags** in the head section?
flashcards.back: **Link tags** connect external resources like CSS files.

flashcards.front: What is the function of **script tags**?
flashcards.back: **Script tags** include or reference JavaScript files.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between **internal styles** and **external styles** in CSS?
flashcards.back: **Internal styles** are defined within a <style> tag in the head section, while **external styles** are linked through a separate CSS file.

flashcards.front: What is the role of **buttons** in forms?
flashcards.back: **Buttons** submit or reset the form.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of **ARIA labels**?
flashcards.back: **ARIA labels** provide additional information for assistive technologies.

title: C.1.9 Protocols and Standards on the Web Flashcards

flashcards.front: What is a **protocol**?
flashcards.back: A **set of rules** that defines how data is transmitted and received over a network.

flashcards.front: What is a **standard**?
flashcards.back: An agreed-upon specification that ensures interoperability between different systems and devices.

flashcards.front: What does **HTTP** stand for?
flashcards.back: **Hypertext Transfer Protocol**.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of **HTTP**?
flashcards.back: It defines how web browsers and servers communicate.

flashcards.front: What is **HTTPS**?
flashcards.back: A secure version of HTTP that **encrypts data**.

flashcards.front: What does **TCP/IP** stand for?
flashcards.back: **Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol**.

flashcards.front: What is the role of **TCP/IP**?
flashcards.back: It is the foundational protocol suite for the internet, ensuring reliable data transmission.

flashcards.front: What does **DNS** do?
flashcards.back: It translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses.

flashcards.front: What is **FTP** used for?
flashcards.back: Transferring files between computers on a network.

flashcards.front: What does **SMTP** handle?
flashcards.back: The sending of emails.

flashcards.front: What is **interoperability** in the context of protocols?
flashcards.back: It ensures that devices and applications from different manufacturers can work together seamlessly.

flashcards.front: How do protocols contribute to **scalability**?
flashcards.back: They allow the web to grow and accommodate more users and devices without compatibility issues.

flashcards.front: What protocols protect data during transmission?
flashcards.back: **HTTPS** and **SSL/TLS**.

flashcards.front: What protocol ensures data is delivered accurately?
How do standards improve **efficiency**?
flashcards.back: They optimize data transmission, reducing latency and improving user experience.

flashcards.front: What organizations develop and maintain web standards?
flashcards.back: The **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)** and the **World Wide Web Consortium (W3C)**.

flashcards.front: What is a challenge related to **evolving standards**?
flashcards.back: Protocols must be **updated** to address new challenges, such as increased security threats.

flashcards.front: What is a challenge related to **adoption** of standards?
flashcards.back: Ensuring that all devices and applications adopt the **latest standards**.

flashcards.front: What is the importance of **backward compatibility**?
flashcards.back: New protocols must be backward-compatible to avoid disrupting existing systems.

flashcards.front: What is the **international impact** of protocols and standards?
flashcards.back: They enable global communication by providing a common "language" for devices worldwide.

title: C.1.10 Types of Web Pages Flashcards

flashcards.front: What are **personal pages**?
flashcards.back: Websites created by individuals to share information about themselves, their interests, or their work.

flashcards.front: What content is typically found on **personal pages**?
flashcards.back: Biographical information, portfolios of work, personal blogs, and photo galleries.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of a **personal portfolio website**?
flashcards.back: To showcase an individual's projects, resume, and contact information.

flashcards.front: What are **blogs**?
flashcards.back: Websites that feature regularly updated content, typically written in an informal or conversational style.

flashcards.front: What topics can **blogs** cover?
flashcards.back: Personal experiences, professional advice, news and commentary, tutorials, and how-to guides.

flashcards.front: What are **search engine pages**?
flashcards.back: Pages designed to help users find information on the internet by indexing and ranking web pages based on relevance to search queries.

flashcards.front: What are the key features of **search engine pages**?
flashcards.back: A search bar for user queries, algorithms that rank results, advertisements, and additional services like image or video search.

flashcards.front: What are **forums**?
flashcards.back: Online discussion platforms where users can post messages, ask questions, and participate in conversations on various topics.

flashcards.front: How are **forums** typically organized?
flashcards.back: Into categories and threads, making it easy to navigate discussions.

flashcards.front: What are common features of **forums**?
flashcards.back: User registration and profiles, moderation tools, search functionality, and private messaging between users.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of **blogs**?
flashcards.back: To provide regularly updated content on various topics.

flashcards.front: What is an example of a **search engine page**?
flashcards.back: Google.

flashcards.front: What is an example of a **forum**?
flashcards.back: Reddit.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between **personal pages** and **blogs**?
flashcards.back: Personal pages share static information about an individual, while blogs provide regularly updated content on various topics.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of **search engine pages**?
flashcards.back: To help users find information online.

title: C.1.11 Static vs. Dynamic Web Pages Flashcards

flashcards.front: What is a **static web page**?
flashcards.back: A web page that is **delivered to the user exactly as stored**, without any **server-side processing** or **customization**.

flashcards.front: What is a **dynamic web page**?
flashcards.back: A web page that is **generated in real-time** by the server, often using **server-side scripts**, and can be **customized** based on user input or other factors.

flashcards.front: How is content **generated** in a **static** web page?
flashcards.back: Content is **fixed** and does **not change** unless the HTML file is **manually updated**.

flashcards.front: How is content **generated** in a **dynamic** web page?
flashcards.back: Content is **generated on-the-fly** based on user input, database queries, or other factors.

flashcards.front: What is the **interactivity** of a **static** web page?
flashcards.back: Limited to client-side scripts like JavaScript, cannot interact with databases or server-side resources.

flashcards.front: What is the **interactivity** of a **dynamic** web page?
flashcards.back: Can interact with databases, user sessions, and other server-side resources.

flashcards.front: Which type of web page is **faster** to load: static or dynamic?
flashcards.back: **Static** web pages are faster to load because the server simply delivers the file.

flashcards.front: Why are **dynamic** web pages **slower** to load?
flashcards.back: Because the server must **process scripts** and **generate the page**.

flashcards.front: Which type of web page is **easier** to **scale**: static or dynamic?
flashcards.back: **Static** web pages are easier to scale because the server load is minimal.

flashcards.front: What are the **maintenance** differences between static and dynamic web pages?
flashcards.back: Static: Difficult to maintain for large websites. Dynamic: Easier to maintain because templates and databases can be used.

flashcards.front: Give an example of a **static web page**.
flashcards.back: A simple HTML file that displays a company's contact information.

flashcards.front: Give an example of a **dynamic web page**.
flashcards.back: A blog where new posts are fetched from a database and displayed to the user.

flashcards.front: What are **static web pages** often used for?
flashcards.back: Simple websites or landing pages.

flashcards.front: What are **dynamic web pages** used for?
flashcards.back: Interactive applications like e-commerce sites or social networks.

flashcards.front: Can **dynamic** mean **slow**?
flashcards.back: **Not necessarily**. With the right optimization and design, dynamic pages can be effective.

flashcards.front: Can **static** mean **boring**?
flashcards.back: **Not necessarily**. With the right design, static pages can be effective for their intended purposes.

title: C.1.12 Functions of a Browser Flashcards

flashcards.front: What is the difference between HTTP and HTTPS?
flashcards.back: HTTPS is the secure version of HTTP, ensuring that data transmitted between the browser and server is encrypted.

flashcards.front: How does a browser render web pages?
flashcards.back: The browser interprets the HTML structure, applies CSS styles, and executes JavaScript for interactivity.

flashcards.front: What is **sandboxing** in browsers?
flashcards.back: Sandboxing isolates web pages to prevent malicious code from affecting your device.

flashcards.front: What are some security threats to browsers?
flashcards.back: Browsers are targets for phishing, malware, and cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks.

flashcards.front: What is the **Document Object Model (DOM)**?
flashcards.back: The DOM is a tree-like structure representing a web page's elements, allowing JavaScript interaction.

flashcards.front: What is the role of a **rendering engine**?
flashcards.back: A rendering engine interprets HTML, CSS, and JavaScript to display web pages.

flashcards.front: What is **lazy loading**?
flashcards.back: Lazy loading is a technique that defers the loading of non-essential resources to improve performance.

flashcards.front: What is the role of a JavaScript engine?
flashcards.back: A JavaScript engine executes JavaScript code to enable interactivity and dynamic content.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between cookies and local storage?
flashcards.back: Cookies are used for session management, while local storage provides offline access and personalized settings.

flashcards.front: What is the significance of **WebAssembly**?
flashcards.back: WebAssembly allows browsers to run high-performance applications, enhancing web capabilities.

flashcards.front: What is **cross-browser compatibility**?
flashcards.back: Ensuring that websites work consistently across different browsers and versions.

flashcards.front: What is the impact of poorly optimized scripts?
flashcards.back: Poorly optimized scripts can slow down browsing experience, causing lag or unresponsive pages.

flashcards.front: What is the role of the networking layer in browsers?
flashcards.back: The networking layer handles communication with web servers using protocols like HTTP and HTTPS.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous scripting?
flashcards.back: Synchronous scripting blocks page loading, while asynchronous scripting allows concurrent execution.

flashcards.front: What is the significance of Progressive Web Apps (PWAs)?
flashcards.back: PWAs enable web applications to function like native apps, providing offline access and push notifications.

flashcards.front: What is the role of data storage in browsers?
flashcards.back: Data storage supports features like session management, offline access, and personalized settings.

flashcards.front: What is the process of layout and painting in browsers?
flashcards.back: The browser calculates the position of elements and paints them onto the screen, layer by layer.

flashcards.front: What are **browser extensions**?
flashcards.back: Extensions are add-ons that provide extra functionality, such as ad blockers or password managers.

flashcards.front: What is **caching** in browsers?
flashcards.back: Caching stores frequently accessed resources locally, reducing load times for subsequent visits.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of browser updates?
flashcards.back: Browser updates provide security patches and protect users from emerging threats.

title: C.1.13 Client-Side vs. Server-Side Scripting Flashcards

flashcards.front: What is **client-side scripting**?
flashcards.back: Code that runs in the user's **web browser**, typically written in **JavaScript**, **HTML**, and **CSS**.

flashcards.front: Where is **client-side scripting** executed?
flashcards.back: On the user's device, reducing the load on the server.

flashcards.front: What is **server-side scripting**?
flashcards.back: Code that runs on the **web server**, using languages like **PHP**, **Python**, **Ruby**, or **Node.js**.

flashcards.front: Where is **server-side scripting** executed?
flashcards.back: On the server, ensuring consistent behavior across all users.

flashcards.front: What is one advantage of **client-side scripting**?
flashcards.back: **Speed**: Reduces server load and provides faster interactivity.

flashcards.front: What is one advantage of **server-side scripting**?
flashcards.back: **Security**: Code is hidden from users, making it ideal for handling sensitive data.

flashcards.front: What is a limitation of **client-side scripting**?
flashcards.back: **Security risks**: Code is visible and modifiable by users.

flashcards.front: What is a limitation of **server-side scripting**?
flashcards.back: **Slower response time**: Requires communication with the server.

flashcards.front: How do modern web applications use **client-side** and **server-side scripting**?
flashcards.back: They use a **combination** of both approaches to leverage their strengths.

flashcards.front: What is a good rule of thumb for using **client-side** and **server-side scripting**?
flashcards.back: Use **client-side scripting** for interactivity and **server-side scripting** for security and data management.

flashcards.front: What is an example of **client-side scripting** use?
flashcards.back: **Form validation**: Provides instant feedback to users.

flashcards.front: What is an example of **server-side scripting** use?
flashcards.back: Ensures **data integrity** and security during form submission.

flashcards.front: What are the **advantages** of **client-side scripting**?
flashcards.back:
1. Speed
2. Interactivity
3. Reduced server load

flashcards.front: What are the **limitations** of **server-side scripting**?
flashcards.back:
1. Slower response time
2. Increased server load
3. Limited interactivity

flashcards.front: What are the **advantages** of **server-side scripting**?
flashcards.back:
1. Security
2. Consistency
3. Database integration

flashcards.front: What are the **limitations** of **client-side scripting**?
flashcards.back:
1. Security risks
2. Browser compatibility
3. Dependency on client resources

title: C.1.14 Web Pages and Data Sources Flashcards

flashcards.front: What is a **dynamic website**?
flashcards.back: A website that generates content **on-the-fly**, allowing for features like user profiles and live updates.

flashcards.front: How do data-driven websites enhance **personalization**?
flashcards.back: They can tailor content to **individual** users, improving the user experience.

flashcards.front: What are the three main components involved in connecting a web page to a data source?
flashcards.back:
1. Client-Side
2. Server-Side
3. Data Source

flashcards.front: What is the role of the **client-side** in web data interaction?
flashcards.back: The client's browser displays the web page and sends requests to the server.

flashcards.front: What **HTTP methods** are commonly used for client-side requests?
flashcards.back: GET and POST.

flashcards.front: What does the server do upon receiving a client request?
flashcards.back: It determines **what data** is needed and interacts with the data source to retrieve or update information.

flashcards.front: What are common **data sources** for web servers?
flashcards.back:
1. Relational Databases
2. NoSQL Databases
3. APIs

flashcards.front: How do servers communicate with **relational databases**?
flashcards.back: Using **SQL** queries.

flashcards.front: In what formats can the server send data back to the client?
flashcards.back: HTML, JSON, or XML.

flashcards.front: What are some **server-side languages** used for handling data interactions?
flashcards.back: PHP, Python, Ruby, and Node.js.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of **AJAX** in web development?
flashcards.back: AJAX enables web pages to update content **without reloading**, providing a smoother user experience.

flashcards.front: What are the **security risks** of connecting web pages to data sources?
flashcards.back: SQL injection and data breaches.

flashcards.front: What **security measures** should developers implement?
flashcards.back: Input validation, encryption, and authentication.

flashcards.front: What is a common mistake that leads to **SQL injection attacks**?
flashcards.back: Failing to sanitize user input.

flashcards.front: What are the **challenges** of connecting web pages to data sources?
flashcards.back:
1. Scalability
2. Latency
3. Data Consistency

flashcards.front: What is **Common Gateway Interface (CGI)**?
flashcards.back: A standard protocol that allows web servers to interact with external programs, often written in languages like **Perl** or **Python**.

flashcards.front: What does CGI enable?
flashcards.back: CGI enables dynamic content generation by executing scripts in response to user requests.

flashcards.front: Where are CGI scripts typically stored?
flashcards.back: CGI scripts are typically stored in a special directory, such as **cgi-bin**, on the web server.

flashcards.front: What is the first step in how CGI works?
flashcards.back: **User Request:** A user submits a form or requests a resource that requires dynamic processing.

flashcards.front: What happens during the **Server Execution** step in CGI?
flashcards.back: The web server identifies the request as a CGI call and executes the corresponding script.

flashcards.front: How does a CGI script process input data?
flashcards.back: The CGI script processes input data, such as form fields, and generates output (e.g., HTML).

flashcards.front: What is the final step in the CGI process?
flashcards.back: **Response:** The server sends the script's output back to the user's browser.

flashcards.front: What is a good analogy for CGI?
flashcards.back: CGI can be thought of as a **translator** that converts user input into actions performed by the server, then translates the results back into a web page.

flashcards.front: What is one advantage of CGI?
flashcards.back: **Simplicity:** Easy to implement with basic scripting knowledge.

flashcards.front: What is another advantage of CGI?
flashcards.back: **Language Flexibility:** Supports multiple programming languages.

flashcards.front: What is a major disadvantage of CGI?
flashcards.back: **Performance:** Each request spawns a new process, leading to high overhead.

flashcards.front: Why is CGI not suitable for high-traffic websites?
flashcards.back: **Scalability:** Not suitable for high-traffic websites due to resource constraints.

flashcards.front: How can a CGI script process form data for GET requests?
flashcards.back: By reading environment variables like **QUERY_STRING**.

flashcards.front: How can a CGI script process form data for POST requests?
flashcards.back: By reading from **stdin**.

flashcards.front: What is **Web Page Structure**?
flashcards.back: The organization and layout of elements on a web page, including headers, navigation menus, content sections, and footers.

flashcards.front: What are the **key components** of a web page?
flashcards.back: Header, Main Content, Sidebar, and Footer.

flashcards.front: What does the **header** of a web page typically contain?
flashcards.back: Logo, Navigation Bar, and Search Bar.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of a **logo** in the header?
flashcards.back: It represents the brand or identity of the website.

flashcards.front: What is included in the **main content** section?
flashcards.back: Text, Images, Videos, and Forms.

flashcards.front: What is the function of **forms** in the main content?
flashcards.back: Interactive elements for user input, such as contact forms or login forms.

flashcards.front: What can be found in the **sidebar**?
flashcards.back: Additional Navigation, Advertisements, and Widgets.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of **widgets** in the sidebar?
flashcards.back: Small applications like weather updates or social media feeds.

flashcards.front: What information is typically in the **footer**?
flashcards.back: Contact Information, Social Media Links, and Legal Information.

flashcards.front: What are **Static Web Pages**?
flashcards.back: Fixed content that does not change unless manually updated.

flashcards.front: What is an example of a **Static Web Page**?
flashcards.back: Personal blogs, portfolio websites.

flashcards.front: What are **Dynamic Web Pages**?
flashcards.back: Content is generated in real-time based on user interactions or data from a database.

flashcards.front: What is an example of a **Dynamic Web Page**?
flashcards.back: E-commerce sites, social media platforms.

flashcards.front: What are **Single Page Applications (SPAs)**?
flashcards.back: Loads a single HTML page and dynamically updates content without refreshing the page.

flashcards.front: What is an example of a **Single Page Application**?
flashcards.back: Gmail, Google Maps.

flashcards.front: What are **Responsive Web Pages**?
flashcards.back: Adapts layout and content to different screen sizes and devices.

flashcards.front: What is an example of a **Responsive Web Page**?
flashcards.back: Modern news websites, e-commerce platforms.

flashcards.front: What factors affect **Web Page Structure**?
flashcards.back: User Experience (UX), Accessibility, and Search Engine Optimization (SEO).

flashcards.front: What are the criteria for **evaluating web page structure**?
flashcards.back: Clarity, Consistency, Flexibility, and Performance.

flashcards.front: What tools can be used for **evaluating web page structure**?
flashcards.back: Google Lighthouse, WAVE, GTmetrix.

flashcards.front: What is a challenge in **web page structure**?
flashcards.back: Balancing aesthetics and functionality.

flashcards.front: What is the importance of **accessibility** in web page structure?
flashcards.back: Ensuring the site is usable for all users, including those with disabilities.

flashcards.front: What is a **search engine**?
flashcards.back: A **software system** that **indexes** and **retrieves** information from a **database** or the **internet** based on **user queries**.

flashcards.front: What do search engines use to **rank** and **display** results?
flashcards.back: Search engines use **algorithms** to rank and display the most relevant results.

flashcards.front: What is the **surface web**?
flashcards.back: The surface web refers to the portion of the internet that is **indexed by search engines** like Google, Bing, or Yahoo.

flashcards.front: What is the **deep web**?
flashcards.back: The deep web encompasses all web content that is **not indexed by search engines**.

flashcards.front: What are the **accessibility** differences between the surface web and the deep web?
flashcards.back: Surface web is **publicly accessible** without restrictions, while deep web **requires authentication** or specific permissions.

flashcards.front: What types of content are found in the **surface web**?
flashcards.back: Static web pages, blogs, news articles.

flashcards.front: What types of content are found in the **deep web**?
flashcards.back: Dynamic content, databases, private information.

flashcards.front: Why does the **deep web** exist?
flashcards.back:
1. Privacy
2. Security
3. Dynamic Content

flashcards.front: What is a common misconception about the **deep web**?
flashcards.back: The deep web is often misunderstood as being synonymous with the dark web.

flashcards.front: What is the **dark web**?
flashcards.back: The dark web is a small, encrypted portion of the deep web used for anonymous activities, both legal and illegal.

flashcards.front: What are the **challenges** of accessing the deep web?
flashcards.back:
1. Authentication Requirements
2. Lack of Indexing
3. Legal and Ethical Concerns

title: C.2.2 Principles of Search Algorithms Flashcards

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of **searching algorithms** in search engines?
flashcards.back: To help users find relevant information on the internet by analyzing and ranking web pages based on their relevance and importance.

flashcards.front: What is **PageRank**?
flashcards.back: A search algorithm developed by Google that ranks web pages based on the number and quality of links pointing to them.

flashcards.front: How does PageRank view links between pages?
flashcards.back: PageRank treats a link from one page to another as a 'vote' of confidence, assuming that a page is important if it is linked to by other important pages.

flashcards.front: What is the **damping factor** in PageRank?
flashcards.back: A factor (usually around 0.85) that represents the probability that a user will continue clicking on links, used to prevent infinite loops in the algorithm.

flashcards.front: What is **HITS**?
flashcards.back: A search algorithm that identifies two types of pages: hubs and authorities.

flashcards.front: What are **hubs** and **authorities** in the HITS algorithm?
flashcards.back: Hubs are pages that link to many authoritative pages, and authorities are pages that are linked to by many hubs.

flashcards.front: How does HITS update the scores of hubs and authorities?
flashcards.back: HITS assigns two scores to each page (hub score and authority score) and updates them iteratively, with hubs boosting authorities and vice versa.

flashcards.front: What is the main difference between PageRank and HITS?
flashcards.back: PageRank focuses on the overall importance of a page based on incoming links using a single score, while HITS differentiates between hubs and authorities using two scores.

flashcards.front: When is HITS particularly useful?
flashcards.back: HITS is useful for specialized, topic-specific searches as it focuses on the relationship between hubs and authorities.

flashcards.front: What computational thinking concepts are involved in search algorithms?
flashcards.back: Abstraction, decomposition, and pattern recognition.

flashcards.front: How do search algorithms use **abstraction**?
flashcards.back: They simplify the web into a graph of nodes (pages) and edges (links) to analyze relationships.

flashcards.front: What is the role of **decomposition** in search algorithms?
flashcards.back: The problem of ranking pages is broken down into smaller tasks, such as calculating scores and updating them iteratively.

flashcards.front: How do search algorithms utilize **pattern recognition**?
flashcards.back: They identify patterns in linking behavior to determine the importance of web pages.

flashcards.front: Is PageRank the only factor in search rankings?
flashcards.back: No, modern search engines use hundreds of signals to determine relevance, with PageRank being just one of them.

flashcards.front: What is the significance of **link analysis** in PageRank?
flashcards.back: PageRank assumes that a page is important if it is linked to by other important pages, using links as 'votes' of confidence.

title: C.2.3 How Web Crawlers Function Flashcards

flashcards.front: What are **web crawlers**?
flashcards.back: Automated programs that systematically browse the internet to collect information from web pages.

flashcards.front: What are other names for **web crawlers**?
flashcards.back: They are also known as **bots**, **spiders**, or **web robots**.

flashcards.front: Why are **web crawlers** essential for search engines?
flashcards.back: They gather data to build and update search indexes.

flashcards.front: What are **seed URLs**?
flashcards.back: Initial web addresses that crawlers begin with, often well-known or frequently updated websites.

flashcards.front: How do crawlers **fetch web pages**?
flashcards.back: By sending **HTTP requests** to seed URLs and receiving the **HTML content** in response.

flashcards.front: What do crawlers **parse** in the HTML content?
flashcards.back: They parse the HTML content to extract useful information, such as text, metadata, and links.

flashcards.front: How do crawlers **navigate** the web?
flashcards.back: By identifying hyperlinks within HTML content and adding them to a list of URLs to visit next.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the **robots.txt** file?
flashcards.back: It specifies which pages should or should not be crawled, allowing websites to control crawler behavior.

flashcards.front: How do crawlers handle **duplicate content**?
flashcards.back: They use algorithms to identify and avoid indexing redundant information.

flashcards.front: What is a challenge faced by web crawlers regarding **scalability**?
flashcards.back: The web is vast and constantly growing, making it challenging for crawlers to keep up.

flashcards.front: How do advanced crawlers handle **dynamic content**?
flashcards.back: They use **headless browsers** to render and extract dynamic content loaded by JavaScript.

flashcards.front: What are **politeness policies** for crawlers?
flashcards.back: Policies that implement waiting between requests or limiting concurrent connections to avoid overloading servers.

flashcards.front: What is one application of web crawlers in **data mining**?
flashcards.back: Businesses use crawlers to gather market data, monitor competitors, or track trends.

flashcards.front: How do web crawlers contribute to **archiving**?
flashcards.back: Organizations like the Internet Archive use crawlers to preserve web content for future reference.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a **web browser** and a **web crawler**?
flashcards.back: A web browser loads pages for user interaction, while a crawler automatically fetches and processes pages at scale.

flashcards.front: What factors do crawlers consider when **prioritizing links** to follow?
flashcards.back: Page importance, update frequency, and relevance.

flashcards.front: What is the impact of **failing to handle duplicate content**?
flashcards.back: It can lead to bloated search indexes and lower search quality.

flashcards.front: What is the role of **HTTP requests** in web crawling?
flashcards.back: Crawlers send HTTP requests to retrieve web pages from servers.

flashcards.front: What is the significance of **HTML content** for crawlers?
flashcards.back: HTML content contains the information that crawlers parse and extract for indexing.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between **web crawlers** and **search indexes**?
flashcards.back: Crawlers collect data that is used to build and update search indexes.

title: C.2.4 Metadata and Web Crawlers Flashcards

flashcards.front: What is **metadata**?
flashcards.back: Data that describes other data, providing information such as content, format, source, or structure to help organize, find, and understand it.

flashcards.front: What does metadata provide in the context of web pages?
flashcards.back: Information about the content of a page, such as its title, description, keywords, and author.

flashcards.front: Where is metadata typically stored in a web page?
flashcards.back: In **meta-tags** within the HTML code of the web page.

flashcards.front: Where are **meta-tags** placed in an HTML document?
flashcards.back: Within the <head> section of the HTML document.

flashcards.front: What are **web crawlers**?
flashcards.back: Automated programs used by search engines to discover and index web pages.

flashcards.front: How do web crawlers access metadata?
flashcards.back: By analyzing the HTML code of a web page to extract information from meta-tags.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of metadata for web crawlers?
flashcards.back: To help crawlers understand the content and context of a page for indexing and ranking.

flashcards.front: How can well-crafted metadata improve search rankings?
flashcards.back: By highlighting relevant keywords and topics, improving a page's visibility in search results.

flashcards.front: What is the function of the **robots meta-tag**?
flashcards.back: To instruct crawlers on whether to index a page or follow its links.

flashcards.front: What is a challenge related to **misleading metadata**?
flashcards.back: Metadata can be manipulated to misrepresent the content of a page, leading to inaccurate search results.

flashcards.front: What is the risk of **over-reliance on metadata** by crawlers?
flashcards.back: Crawlers may miss important information if they rely too heavily on incomplete or poorly written metadata.

flashcards.front: Why is **dynamic content** a challenge for web crawlers?
flashcards.back: Pages with dynamic or user-generated content may not have accurate metadata, making it difficult to index them.

flashcards.front: What is a common misconception about **keywords meta-tag**?
flashcards.back: That adding irrelevant keywords will improve search rankings.

flashcards.front: How have search engines responded to **keyword stuffing**?
flashcards.back: They have become sophisticated and can penalize pages for keyword stuffing.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between **metadata** and **web crawlers**?
flashcards.back: Metadata provides essential information that helps web crawlers understand the content of a page without analyzing the entire text.

flashcards.front: What are **web crawlers**?
flashcards.back: Automated programs that systematically browse the internet to collect information from web pages.

flashcards.front: How do **traditional web crawlers** operate?
flashcards.back: They operate **sequentially**, processing one web page at a time.

flashcards.front: Why has **sequential crawling** become inefficient?
flashcards.back: Because the web has grown **exponentially**, making sequential crawling too slow.

flashcards.front: What is **parallel web crawling**?
flashcards.back: A technique that allows multiple crawlers to work **simultaneously**.

flashcards.front: How does **parallel web crawling** work?
flashcards.back: Multiple crawler instances run **concurrently**, each responsible for a subset of the web.

flashcards.front: Where can parallel crawlers operate?
flashcards.back: They can operate on **different machines** or within a **distributed system**.

flashcards.front: How is the web divided for parallel crawling?
flashcards.back: The web is divided into **segments**, and each crawler is assigned a specific segment.

flashcards.front: What criteria can be used for web segment division?
flashcards.back: Criteria such as domain, URL patterns, or geographical location.

flashcards.front: How do crawlers **coordinate** to avoid duplication?
flashcards.back: Through **shared data structures** and communication protocols.

flashcards.front: What is used for coordination among crawlers?
flashcards.back: Shared data structures, such as distributed hash tables.

flashcards.front: How is **load balancing** achieved in parallel crawling?
flashcards.back: If one crawler finishes early, it can be reassigned to assist with other segments.

flashcards.front: What is one benefit of **increased speed** in parallel crawling?
flashcards.back: By processing multiple web pages simultaneously, parallel crawlers can index the web much faster.

flashcards.front: How does parallel crawling provide **scalability**?
flashcards.back: Parallel crawling systems can scale by **adding more crawlers**.

flashcards.front: What is a benefit of **fault tolerance** in parallel crawling?
flashcards.back: If one crawler fails, others can continue working, ensuring the process is not disrupted.

flashcards.front: What is a challenge of **coordination** in parallel crawling?
flashcards.back: Managing multiple crawlers requires sophisticated coordination mechanisms to prevent duplication.

flashcards.front: What **resource management** challenges exist in parallel crawling?
flashcards.back: Parallel crawlers consume more resources, such as bandwidth and storage, requiring efficient management.

flashcards.front: What must crawlers respect to ensure **politeness**?
flashcards.back: Crawlers must respect website policies, such as robots.txt.

flashcards.front: How do crawlers avoid **overloading servers**?
flashcards.back: By **throttling requests** to avoid overloading servers.

title: C.2.6 Web-Indexing in Search Engines Flashcards

flashcards.front: What is **web indexing**?
flashcards.back: A critical process that enables search engines to **organize** and **retrieve information efficiently** from the vast expanse of the internet.

flashcards.front: What is the first step in web-indexing?
flashcards.back: **Crawling**, where search engines use automated programs called web crawlers or spiders to navigate the web.

flashcards.front: What do web crawlers do?
flashcards.back: They **visit web pages**, follow links, and **collect data** about the content and structure of each page.

flashcards.front: What happens after crawling?
flashcards.back: The search engine **analyzes the collected data** to extract meaningful information, such as identifying keywords and understanding context.

flashcards.front: What is the role of **metadata** in web-indexing?
flashcards.back: Metadata provides additional context that helps search engines **understand the relevance** of a page.

flashcards.front: Where is the extracted information stored?
flashcards.back: In a **search index**, a massive database that organizes data for quick retrieval.

flashcards.front: What details does the search index include?
flashcards.back: The location of keywords, the frequency of their occurrence, and the relationships between different pages.

flashcards.front: How does web-indexing enable efficient information retrieval?
flashcards.back: Search engines can retrieve relevant results in a **fraction of a second** by looking up the index instead of searching the entire web.

flashcards.front: What is an example of efficient information retrieval?
flashcards.back: When searching for "best pizza recipes," the search engine quickly scans its index for pages containing those keywords.

flashcards.front: How does web-indexing improve search accuracy?
flashcards.back: By analyzing content and structure, search engines can **prioritize pages** that are most likely to satisfy the user's query.

flashcards.front: What is a challenge in web-indexing related to **dynamic content**?
flashcards.back: Many websites generate content dynamically, making it challenging for crawlers to **access and index** all relevant information.

flashcards.front: What is the issue with **duplicate content** in web-indexing?
flashcards.back: Search engines must ensure that the index does not contain redundant pages, which can affect search quality and efficiency.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of **canonical tags**?
flashcards.back: Canonical tags help webmasters indicate the **preferred version of a page**, reducing the risk of duplicate content in the index.

flashcards.front: What are the privacy and security challenges in web-indexing?
flashcards.back: Some web pages are restricted by robots.txt files or require authentication, limiting the ability of crawlers to **access and index** them.

flashcards.front: What is the role of **robots.txt** files?
flashcards.back: Robots.txt files restrict web crawlers from accessing certain web pages, ensuring privacy and security.

flashcards.front: Why is web-indexing essential for **scalability**?
flashcards.back: Web-indexing allows search engines to **scale efficiently** by organizing vast amounts of data in a structured manner.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between **crawling** and **analyzing** in web-indexing?
flashcards.back: Crawling is the process of **collecting data** from web pages, while analyzing is the process of **extracting meaningful information** from that data.

flashcards.front: How do search engines **rank indexed pages**?
flashcards.back: Search engines use complex algorithms that consider factors such as keyword relevance, page authority, and user engagement.

flashcards.front: What is the significance of **user engagement** in search ranking?
flashcards.back: User engagement is a factor that search engines consider when **ranking indexed pages** to determine their relevance and quality.

flashcards.front: What is **Search Engine Optimization (SEO)**?
flashcards.back: The process of optimizing web pages to improve their ranking in search engine results.

flashcards.front: What are two key aspects of **keyword optimization** in SEO?
flashcards.back:
1. Use relevant keywords in titles, headings, and content.
2. Avoid keyword stuffing.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of **meta tags** in SEO?
flashcards.back: Meta tags help search engines understand the page's content.

flashcards.front: What are the characteristics of **content quality** for SEO?
flashcards.back: Content should be original, informative, and engaging.

flashcards.front: What are **backlinks** and why are they important?
flashcards.back: Backlinks from reputable websites signal authority and trustworthiness.

flashcards.front: How can **social signals** boost SEO?
flashcards.back: Shares, likes, and comments on social media can increase content visibility.

flashcards.front: What is **mobile optimization** in technical SEO?
flashcards.back: Ensuring the website is responsive and works well on mobile devices.

flashcards.front: Why is **site speed** important for SEO?
flashcards.back: Faster websites provide a better user experience and rank higher.

flashcards.front: What is the significance of **secure connections** in SEO?
flashcards.back: Search engines favor websites that use HTTPS over non-secure ones.

flashcards.front: What is **schema markup**?
flashcards.back: Schema markup is used to help search engines understand the content.

flashcards.front: How can schema markup enhance search results?
flashcards.back: Schema can provide rich snippets, such as ratings or event details.

flashcards.front: What is one way to improve **user experience** on a website?
flashcards.back: Use clear menus and internal links for easy navigation.

flashcards.front: What does **engaging content** include?
flashcards.back: Engaging content includes images, videos, and interactive elements.

flashcards.front: What tool can be used to **monitor user behavior** on a website?
flashcards.back: Google Analytics.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of **Google Search Console**?
flashcards.back: To monitor rankings and traffic of web pages.

flashcards.front: Why is **continuous improvement** important in SEO?
flashcards.back: SEO is an ongoing process that requires regular updates to content and strategies.

flashcards.front: What can result from **ignoring SEO**?
flashcards.back: Ignoring SEO can result in low visibility, even for high-quality content.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between **on-page SEO** and **off-page SEO**?
flashcards.back: On-page SEO focuses on optimizing elements within the website, while off-page SEO involves external factors like backlinks and social signals.

flashcards.front: What is a **tip** for finding relevant keywords?
flashcards.back: Use tools like Google Keyword Planner.

flashcards.front: What is a **note** about technical SEO?
flashcards.back: Technical SEO is often overlooked but is crucial for improving search rankings.

flashcards.front: What is an **example** of schema markup?
flashcards.back: A recipe page with schema markup may display cooking time and ratings directly in search results.

flashcards.front: What is a **tip** for improving user experience?
flashcards.back: Use tools like Google Analytics to monitor user behavior and identify areas for improvement.

title: C.2.8 Metrics Used by Search Engines Flashcards

flashcards.front: What are **On-Page Metrics**?
flashcards.back: Factors found on the web page itself, such as keywords, content quality, and metadata.

flashcards.front: What are **Off-Page Metrics**?
flashcards.back: External factors like backlinks, social signals, and domain authority.

flashcards.front: What are **User Engagement Metrics**?
flashcards.back: Data on how users interact with the page, such as click-through rates and bounce rates.

flashcards.front: What is **Keyword Density**?
flashcards.back: The frequency of keywords in the content.

flashcards.front: What are **Meta Tags**?
flashcards.back: Descriptions and titles that summarize the page's content.

flashcards.front: What does **Content Quality** refer to?
flashcards.back: The relevance, originality, and depth of the content.

flashcards.front: What are **Backlinks**?
flashcards.back: Links from other websites pointing to the page.

flashcards.front: What is **Domain Authority**?
flashcards.back: A score that predicts how well a website will rank.

flashcards.front: What are **Social Signals**?
flashcards.back: Shares, likes, and mentions on social media platforms.

flashcards.front: What is **Click-Through Rate (CTR)**?
flashcards.back: The percentage of users who click on a search result.

flashcards.front: What is **Bounce Rate**?
flashcards.back: The percentage of users who leave the page without interacting.

flashcards.front: What does **Time on Page** measure?
flashcards.back: The average time users spend on the page.

flashcards.front: What is a challenge related to **manipulation** of metrics?
flashcards.back: Some websites use tactics like keyword stuffing or buying backlinks to game the system.

flashcards.front: What **privacy** concerns are associated with metrics?
flashcards.back: User engagement metrics may raise privacy concerns.

flashcards.front: What **bias** might metrics have?
flashcards.back: Metrics may favor larger, established websites over smaller ones.

flashcards.front: What is the risk of **relying too heavily on a single metric**?
flashcards.back: It can lead to inaccurate rankings.

flashcards.front: What is **search engine effectiveness**?
flashcards.back: How well a search engine retrieves **relevant results** based on a query.

flashcards.front: What are **assumptions in development** of search engines?
flashcards.back: The underlying **rules** and **heuristics** used to design search algorithms.

flashcards.front: What is a common assumption regarding **keyword matching**?
flashcards.back: That matching keywords in the query to keywords in documents will yield **relevant results**.

flashcards.front: What is a limitation of keyword matching?
flashcards.back: It may miss documents that use **synonyms** or **related terms**.

flashcards.front: What does **popularity-based ranking** assume?
flashcards.back: That **popular pages** are more relevant.

flashcards.front: What is a limitation of popularity-based ranking?
flashcards.back: **Niche** or **new content** may be overlooked.

flashcards.front: How do some search engines predict **user intent**?
flashcards.back: By using **machine learning**.

flashcards.front: What is a limitation of predicting user intent?
flashcards.back: **Misinterpreting intent** can lead to irrelevant results.

flashcards.front: What is **precision** in search engine metrics?
flashcards.back: The proportion of **retrieved documents** that are relevant.

flashcards.front: What is **recall** in search engine metrics?
flashcards.back: The proportion of **relevant documents** that are retrieved.

flashcards.front: What is the trade-off between **precision** and **recall**?
flashcards.back: Increasing precision may **reduce recall** and vice versa.

flashcards.front: What is an example of **precision**?
flashcards.back: If a search engine returns 10 results and 8 are relevant, the precision is **80%**.

flashcards.front: What is an example of **recall**?
flashcards.back: If there are 20 relevant documents and the engine returns 10 of them, the recall is **50%**.

flashcards.front: What is a challenge related to **ambiguity** in search queries?
flashcards.back: Queries with multiple meanings (e.g., "apple") require **context-aware algorithms**.

flashcards.front: What is a challenge related to **scalability**?
flashcards.back: Handling large datasets while maintaining **speed** and **accuracy**.

flashcards.front: What is a challenge related to **bias** in search algorithms?
flashcards.back: Algorithms may favor certain types of content, leading to **biased results**.

title: C.2.10 White Hat and Black Hat SEO Flashcards

flashcards.front: What is **white hat SEO**?
flashcards.back: **Ethical** practices that align with search engine guidelines, focusing on **providing value** to users and ensuring **long-term success**.

flashcards.front: What is **black hat SEO**?
flashcards.back: **Manipulative** tactics that violate search engine guidelines, often leading to **penalties** or **bans**.

flashcards.front: What is a key technique of **white hat SEO**?
flashcards.back: **Quality Content**: Creating informative, relevant, and engaging content that addresses users' needs.

flashcards.front: How should keywords be used in **white hat SEO**?
flashcards.back: **Keyword Optimization**: Using keywords naturally within content, titles, and meta descriptions.

flashcards.front: What is a **white hat SEO** approach to backlink building?
flashcards.back: **Earning links** from reputable websites through guest posts, partnerships, and high-quality content.

flashcards.front: What does **technical SEO** in white hat practices involve?
flashcards.back: **Improving site speed**, mobile-friendliness, and ensuring proper indexing.

flashcards.front: What is a common **black hat SEO** technique?
flashcards.back: **Keyword Stuffing**: Overloading content with keywords to manipulate rankings.

flashcards.front: What is **cloaking** in black hat SEO?
flashcards.back: **Showing different content** to search engines and users.

flashcards.front: What are **link farms**?
flashcards.back: **Creating networks of low-quality websites** to generate artificial backlinks.

flashcards.front: What is an example of **hidden text** in black hat SEO?
flashcards.back: **Using invisible text filled with keywords** to boost relevance.

flashcards.front: What is the **ethical consideration** for white hat SEO?
flashcards.back: Focuses on **long-term growth** and user satisfaction, aligning with ethical standards and search engine guidelines.

flashcards.front: What is the **risk** of using black hat SEO?
flashcards.back: **Damaging a website's reputation** and visibility due to short-term gains over user experience.

flashcards.front: How do white hat and black hat SEO practices impact **long-term success**?
flashcards.back: White hat SEO focuses on sustainable growth, while black hat SEO risks penalties that can harm a website's visibility.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between **white hat** and **black hat SEO**?
flashcards.back: White hat SEO uses **ethical** practices, while black hat SEO uses **manipulative** tactics.

flashcards.front: What is the **benefit** of white hat SEO?
flashcards.back: Builds a **sustainable online presence** through ethical practices.

flashcards.front: What is the **consequence** of black hat SEO?
flashcards.back: Can result in **severe penalties**, including removal from search engine indexes.

title: C.2.11 Challenges for Growing Search Engines Flashcards

flashcards.front: What is a key challenge for search engines regarding **data volume**?
flashcards.back: Search engines must scale their **infrastructure** to **handle the growth** of billions of web pages being added every year.

flashcards.front: Why is **indexing speed** important for search engines?
flashcards.back: Keeping the index up-to-date is crucial, and search engines need to crawl and index new content quickly **without overloading their servers**.

flashcards.front: What is required for **query processing** as the number of users increases?
flashcards.back: Search engines must process more queries simultaneously, requiring **efficient algorithms** and **powerful hardware**.

flashcards.front: What challenge do search engines face regarding **spam and low-quality content**?
flashcards.back: Search engines must develop algorithms to **filter out irrelevant** or **misleading information**.

flashcards.front: How do search engines address the challenge of **misinformation**?
flashcards.back: Search engines need to identify and **prioritize reliable sources** to ensure the accuracy of search results.

flashcards.front: What is the issue with **content duplication** in search results?
flashcards.back: Duplicate content can clutter search results, so search engines must **detect** and **consolidate similar pages**.

flashcards.front: What are the **privacy** challenges for search engines?
flashcards.back: Search engines collect vast amounts of user data to personalize results, raising concerns about user data protection and ethical considerations.

flashcards.front: What is a key challenge related to **user data protection**?
flashcards.back: Balancing personalization with user privacy is a complex ethical issue.

flashcards.front: What new technology requires search engines to understand **natural language queries**?
flashcards.back: The rise of **voice search** and voice-activated assistants.

flashcards.front: What is the impact of **mobile optimization** on search engines?
flashcards.back: Search engines must prioritize mobile-friendly content as more users access the web via mobile devices.

flashcards.front: What are the challenges of integrating **artificial intelligence** in search engines?
flashcards.back: AI can enhance search algorithms but also introduces challenges such as **bias** and **transparency**.

flashcards.front: What is the challenge of **multilingual support** for search engines?
flashcards.back: The web is becoming more diverse, with content in multiple languages, requiring search engines to support multilingual content.

flashcards.front: How do **cultural differences** affect search algorithms?
flashcards.back: Search algorithms need to account for cultural variations in search behavior.

flashcards.front: What is the **environmental impact** of large-scale data centers?
flashcards.back: Large-scale data centers consume **significant energy**, and search engines must find ways to reduce their carbon footprint.

flashcards.front: What is required for search engines to remain effective as the web **grows**?
flashcards.back: Search engines must address challenges related to scalability, quality assurance, privacy, adaptation to new technologies, globalization, and environmental impact.

flashcards.front: What does addressing search engine challenges require?
flashcards.back: Ongoing **innovation** in technology, infrastructure, and ethical practices.

title: C.3.1 Mobile and Distributed Computing Flashcards

flashcards.front: What is **mobile computing**?
flashcards.back: The ability to access and process data using **portable devices** like smartphones and tablets.

flashcards.front: What are the **key features** of mobile computing?
flashcards.back:
1. Portability
2. Wireless connectivity
3. Battery dependency
4. Resource constraints.

flashcards.front: What is **ubiquitous computing**?
flashcards.back: The concept of embedding computing technology into everyday objects and environments for seamless integration.

flashcards.front: What are the **advantages** of mobile computing?
flashcards.back:
1. Flexibility: Users can work from any location.
2. Real-time access: Immediate access to information and services.

flashcards.front: What are the **challenges** of mobile computing?
flashcards.back:
1. Security risks: Mobile devices are prone to theft and unauthorized access.
2. Connectivity issues: Performance depends on network availability.

flashcards.front: What is an example of mobile computing?
flashcards.back: Using a smartphone to check emails, navigate with GPS, or stream videos while on the move.

flashcards.front: What are the **key features** of ubiquitous computing?
flashcards.back:
1. Invisibility
2. Context-awareness
3. Interconnected devices.

flashcards.front: What are the **advantages** of ubiquitous computing?
flashcards.back:
1. Automation: Reduces manual intervention in daily tasks.
2. Personalization: Systems adapt to individual preferences.

flashcards.front: What are the **challenges** of ubiquitous computing?
flashcards.back:
1. Privacy concerns: Continuous data collection can lead to privacy issues.
2. Complexity: Integrating diverse devices requires robust infrastructure.

flashcards.front: What is an example of ubiquitous computing?
flashcards.back: Smart homes where lights, thermostats, and security systems are controlled automatically based on user preferences.

flashcards.front: What is a **peer-to-peer (P2P) network**?
flashcards.back: A decentralized network where each participant acts as both a client and a server, sharing resources directly.

flashcards.front: What are the **key features** of P2P networks?
flashcards.back:
1. Decentralization
2. Resource sharing
3. Scalability.

flashcards.front: What are the **advantages** of P2P networks?
flashcards.back:
1. Cost-effective: Reduces the need for centralized infrastructure.
2. Fault tolerance: Network remains operational even if some peers fail.

flashcards.front: What are the **challenges** of P2P networks?
flashcards.back:
1. Security risks: Lack of central control can lead to vulnerabilities.
2. Data consistency: Ensuring data integrity across peers can be challenging.

flashcards.front: What is **grid computing**?
flashcards.back: Connecting multiple computers to work together as a single virtual supercomputer for large-scale computational problems.

flashcards.front: What are the **key features** of grid computing?
flashcards.back:
1. Resource aggregation
2. Task parallelism
3. Heterogeneity.

flashcards.front: What are the **advantages** of grid computing?
flashcards.back:
1. High performance: Handles tasks that require massive computational power.
2. Cost savings: Utilizes existing resources instead of investing in supercomputers.

flashcards.front: What are the **challenges** of grid computing?
flashcards.back:
1. Complex management: Coordinating resources across multiple locations is challenging.
2. Security and privacy: Sensitive data may be exposed during processing.

flashcards.front: What is an example of grid computing?
flashcards.back: Scientific research projects like SETI@home, where volunteers contribute their computer's idle processing power.

flashcards.front: What is the focus of **mobile computing** compared to **ubiquitous computing**?
flashcards.back: Mobile computing focuses on **portability**, while ubiquitous computing emphasizes **seamless integration** into daily life.

flashcards.front: What are the **real-world applications** of mobile computing?
flashcards.back: Used in healthcare for remote patient monitoring and in logistics for tracking shipments.

flashcards.front: What are the **real-world applications** of ubiquitous computing?
flashcards.back: Smart cities use sensors for traffic management and energy optimization.

flashcards.front: What are the **real-world applications** of P2P networks?
flashcards.back: Popular in file sharing, blockchain technology, and collaborative platforms.

flashcards.front: What are the **real-world applications** of grid computing?
flashcards.back: Essential in scientific research, weather forecasting, and financial modeling.

flashcards.front: What are the **challenges and considerations** for mobile and distributed computing?
flashcards.back:
1. Security and privacy
2. Scalability
3. Interoperability.

title: C.3.2 Interoperability and Open Standards Flashcards

flashcards.front: What is **interoperability**?
flashcards.back: The ability of different systems, devices, or applications to **work together** and **exchange information seamlessly**.

flashcards.front: What are the key features of interoperability?
flashcards.back:
1. **Data Exchange**: Systems can share and interpret data without loss.
2. **Compatibility**: Different hardware and software work together smoothly.
3. **Scalability**: New systems can be integrated without significant changes.

flashcards.front: What is an example of interoperability in email?
flashcards.back: **Email Protocols**: SMTP, IMAP, and POP3 enable different email clients to communicate.

flashcards.front: What are the benefits of interoperability?
flashcards.back:
1. **Efficiency**: Reduces the need for manual data conversion.
2. **Cost Savings**: Lowers maintenance and development costs.
3. **User Convenience**: Enhances user experience.

flashcards.front: What are the challenges of interoperability?
flashcards.back:
1. **Legacy Systems**: Older systems may not support modern protocols.
2. **Security Risks**: Increased connectivity can expose vulnerabilities.
3. **Complexity**: Ensuring interoperability across diverse systems.

flashcards.front: What are **open standards**?
flashcards.back: **Publicly available specifications** that ensure compatibility and interoperability between different systems.

flashcards.front: What are the key features of open standards?
flashcards.back:
1. **Accessibility**: Open standards are freely available.
2. **Transparency**: The development process is open.
3. **Vendor Neutrality**: No single company has control.

flashcards.front: What is an example of an open standard?
flashcards.back: **HTML**: The standard markup language for web pages, ensuring they can be viewed on any browser.

flashcards.front: What are the benefits of open standards?
flashcards.back:
1. **Interoperability**: Ensures systems can work together.
2. **Innovation**: Encourages development.
3. **Cost Reduction**: Eliminates the need for proprietary solutions.

flashcards.front: What are the challenges of open standards?
flashcards.back:
1. **Adoption**: Not all organizations are willing to adopt open standards.
2. **Fragmentation**: Multiple competing standards can emerge.
3. **Security**: Open standards are accessible to everyone.

flashcards.front: How do interoperability and open standards differ?
flashcards.back:
| Aspect | Interoperability | Open Standards |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Definition | Ability of systems to work together | Publicly available specifications |
| Focus | Seamless communication | Ensuring compatibility |
| Dependency | Often relies on open standards | Facilitates interoperability |
| Control | Can be achieved through proprietary solutions | Always open and accessible |

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between **interoperability** and **open standards**?
flashcards.back: Interoperability is the **goal**, while open standards are the **means** to achieve it.

flashcards.front: Can proprietary systems be interoperable?
flashcards.back: Yes, proprietary systems can be interoperable, but open standards make it easier and more scalable.

flashcards.front: What is a prime example of interoperability achieved through open standards?
flashcards.back: The **Internet**.

flashcards.front: What protocols ensure internet interoperability?
flashcards.back: **TCP/IP**, **HTTP**, and **DNS**.

flashcards.front: What does **TCP/IP** do?
flashcards.back: Enables data transmission across networks.

flashcards.front: What is the role of **HTTP**?
flashcards.back: Facilitates web browsing by defining how messages are formatted and transmitted.

flashcards.front: What does **DNS** do?
flashcards.back: Translates domain names into IP addresses, allowing users to access websites easily.

title: C.3.3 Hardware for Distributed Networks Flashcards

flashcards.front: What are **Network Interface Cards (NICs)**?
flashcards.back: NICs are essential for connecting computers to a network and can be wired (Ethernet) or wireless (Wi-Fi).

flashcards.front: What layers of the OSI model do NICs handle?
flashcards.back: NICs handle the **physical** and **data link** layers, ensuring correct data transmission between devices.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between **routers** and **switches**?
flashcards.back: Routers direct data packets between different networks, while switches manage data flow within a single network.

flashcards.front: How do routers determine the best path for data?
flashcards.back: Routers use **routing tables** to determine the best path for data.

flashcards.front: What do switches use to forward data to the correct device?
flashcards.back: Switches use **MAC addresses** to forward data.

flashcards.front: What are **servers**?
flashcards.back: Servers are powerful computers that provide resources, services, or data to other devices (clients) on the network.

flashcards.front: What are some types of servers?
flashcards.back: Types of servers include **web servers**, **file servers**, and **database servers**.

flashcards.front: What storage solutions are used in distributed networks?
flashcards.back: Distributed networks use **Network Attached Storage (NAS)** and **Storage Area Networks (SAN)**.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of **Wireless Access Points (WAPs)**?
flashcards.back: WAPs enable wireless devices to connect to the network.

flashcards.front: What protocols do WAPs use for better performance?
flashcards.back: WAPs use protocols like **Wi-Fi 6** to provide faster speeds and better coverage.

flashcards.front: What are **firewalls**?
flashcards.back: Firewalls are security devices that monitor and control incoming and outgoing network traffic.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between hardware and software firewalls?
flashcards.back: Hardware firewalls are used at the network perimeter, while software firewalls protect individual devices.

flashcards.front: What has driven the growth of distributed networks?
flashcards.back: The rise of **mobile devices** like smartphones and tablets.

flashcards.front: What connectivity methods do mobile devices rely on?
flashcards.back: Mobile devices rely on **cellular networks**, **Wi-Fi**, and **Bluetooth**.

flashcards.front: What are **edge devices**?
flashcards.back: Edge devices process data closer to the source, reducing latency and bandwidth usage.

flashcards.front: What are some examples of edge devices?
flashcards.back: Examples include **IoT sensors**, **smart cameras**, and **industrial controllers**.

flashcards.front: What types of cables are used for physical connections in distributed networks?
flashcards.back: Cables like **Ethernet**, **fiber optic**, and **coaxial** are used.

flashcards.front: Why are fiber optic cables preferred for long-distance connections?
flashcards.back: Fiber optic cables are preferred due to their high bandwidth and low signal loss.

flashcards.front: What is **Power over Ethernet (PoE)**?
flashcards.back: PoE allows devices to receive power and data through a single cable.

flashcards.front: What is a common use of PoE?
flashcards.back: PoE is commonly used in smart buildings to power devices like lighting systems and security cameras.

title: C.3.4 Role of Distributed Systems in Web Growth Flashcards

flashcards.front: What are the challenges faced by the **centralized web**?
flashcards.back:
1. **Single points of failure**
2. **Limited scalability**
3. **Concerns over privacy and control**

flashcards.front: How do **distributed systems** contribute to a **decentralized web**?
flashcards.back: They spread power across many independent nodes, reducing reliance on centralized entities.

flashcards.front: What is a key characteristic of **distributed systems**?
flashcards.back: They operate **without a single point of control**, making them inherently decentralized.

flashcards.front: How do **peer-to-peer (P2P) networks** support decentralization?
flashcards.back: P2P networks allow nodes to communicate directly without intermediaries, eliminating the need for central servers.

flashcards.front: What is the role of **redundancy** in distributed systems?
flashcards.back: Redundancy ensures data and services are replicated across multiple nodes, maintaining operation even if some nodes fail.

flashcards.front: How do distributed systems enhance **privacy**?
flashcards.back:
1. **Encrypting data**
2. **Reducing attack surfaces** by eliminating central points of attack.

flashcards.front: What is an example of a distributed system?
flashcards.back: **BitTorrent**, which enables file sharing directly between users without a central server.

flashcards.front: How do distributed systems **foster innovation**?
flashcards.back: By allowing **open participation** and **global reach**, lowering barriers to entry.

flashcards.front: What is a challenge of **coordination** in distributed systems?
flashcards.back: Ensuring consistency and agreement among nodes can be complex and resource-intensive.

flashcards.front: What are **consensus algorithms**?
flashcards.back: Protocols like **Proof of Work** or **Proof of Stake** used to achieve agreement in blockchain networks.

flashcards.front: What is a misconception about **decentralization**?
flashcards.back: That decentralization means **no control**. Distributed systems often use governance models to maintain order.

flashcards.front: How do distributed systems **break centralized control**?
flashcards.back:
1. **Distributing data** across multiple nodes
2. **Enabling direct interactions** between users.

flashcards.front: What is the impact of **scalability** on distributed systems?
flashcards.back: Maintaining performance and scalability becomes challenging as distributed systems grow.

flashcards.front: What is the effect of **decentralization** on regulation?
flashcards.back: The decentralized nature complicates regulation, as there is no central entity to hold accountable.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between **distributed systems** and **blockchain**?
flashcards.back: Blockchain is a distributed ledger technology that exemplifies how distributed systems drive decentralization.

title: 1.1.1 Planning for a New System Flashcards

flashcards.front: What is **hardware**?
flashcards.back: The physical parts of a computer system, including internal components and external devices.

flashcards.front: What is **software**?
flashcards.back: A set of programs, instructions, or data that directs computer operation.

flashcards.front: What is a **peripheral**?
flashcards.back: An external device connected to a computer that adds functionality.

flashcards.front: What is a **network**?
flashcards.back: A system where multiple computers or devices are connected to share data and resources.

flashcards.front: What are **human resources** in the context of systems?
flashcards.back: The people who work within an organization and the department that manages hiring, training, and support.

flashcards.front: Who are **stakeholders**?
flashcards.back: People or groups affected by or interested in a new system, including users, clients, developers, managers, and investors.

flashcards.front: What is the importance of **planning** for a new system?
flashcards.back: It ensures alignment with organizational goals, identifies risks, and ensures stakeholder buy-in.

flashcards.front: What are the **benefits** of a new system?
flashcards.back: Improved efficiency, better user experience, enhanced capacity, cost efficiency, future proofing, and better compliance.

flashcards.front: What is **usability**?
flashcards.back: A metric of how the technology or system suits **everybody**.

flashcards.front: What is **accessibility**?
flashcards.back: A metric of how the technology or system suits people with some sort of **disability**.

flashcards.front: What are some methods to improve system **accessibility**?
flashcards.back: Touch screen, voice recognition, text-to-speech, Braille keyboard.

flashcards.front: What are the **primary stages** in the system life cycle?
flashcards.back: Analysis, Design, Implementation/Operation, Installation, Maintenance.

flashcards.front: What is the focus of the **analysis** stage in the system life cycle?
flashcards.back: Understanding the context, including what users need and how the current system works.

flashcards.front: What does the **design** stage in the system life cycle involve?
flashcards.back: Making detailed plans for software and hardware components.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the **implementation/operation** stage?
flashcards.back: Actually realizing the system, such as programming.

flashcards.front: What does the **installation** stage entail?
flashcards.back: Installing and launching the system.

flashcards.front: What is the focus of the **maintenance** stage?
flashcards.back: Providing support, such as updating the system.

flashcards.front: What are the two central considerations when **understanding the context** for a new system?
flashcards.back: Extent (what the system should achieve) and Limitations/Feasibility (potential issues and available resources).

flashcards.front: What is the **TELOS** framework used for?
flashcards.back: Conducting feasibility studies for new systems.

flashcards.front: What does **Technical feasibility** assess?
flashcards.back: Whether the system can be built.

flashcards.front: What does **Economic feasibility** assess?
flashcards.back: Whether the system can be afforded.

flashcards.front: What does **Legal feasibility** assess?
flashcards.back: Whether the system is legal.

flashcards.front: What does **Operational feasibility** assess?
flashcards.back: Whether the system can be implemented into operations.

flashcards.front: What does **Schedule feasibility** assess?
flashcards.back: Whether the system can be developed in time.

flashcards.front: What are the **elements of systems**?
flashcards.back: Hardware, Software, Peripheral, Network, Human Resources, Stakeholders.

flashcards.front: What is a key consideration when **installing** a new system?
flashcards.back: Potential organizational issues such as disruption to workflows, training requirements, resistance to change, and integration with existing systems.

title: 1.1.2 The Importance of Change Management Flashcards

flashcards.front: What is **Change Management**?
